Environmental Impact Statement, Lead Content in Paint Volume 1 and 2

The Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) proposed new regulations to limit the amount of lead in consumer paints to 0.06% to protect children from lead poisoning. This regulation would affect paints used on toys, furniture, and other consumer products. The goal is to reduce the risk of lead exposure, particularly for children who might ingest paint chips. The regulation is expected to have a positive health impact, though it may increase costs for paint manufacturers who need to reformulate their products. Some industries, like automotive and industrial equipment, are concerned about the economic impact and are seeking exemptions.
Department of Health, Education, and Welfare

The proposed regulations by the CPSC aimed to address the serious health risks associated with lead exposure, particularly in children. There was notable pushback from certain industries concerned about the economic implications of reformulating their products to meet the new standards. Ultimately, the regulations were implemented to enhance public health and safety, despite the concerns raised.
"The problem of childhood lead poisoning caused by the ingestion of lead-based paints has reached epidemic proportions in most of our large cities." — Senate Report No. 91-1432

"Children do not get exposed to mirror backing paint; and, therefore, there is no reason to prohibit the use of lead in mirror backing paint." — James E. Mack, National Association of Mirror Manufacturers
